coredata.dc:description: Utilizing biomass-derived carbon materials to enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity of bismuth-based catalysts for tetracycline hydrochloride is significant for the efficient utilization of renewable energy. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) were introduced into Bi2MoO6 via a solvothermal approach to construct the N-CDs/Bi2MoO6 (NCBMO) nanoflower spherical photocatalyst, which is abundant in active sites, constructed electron transfer channels at the interface between N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 by forming Bi-O-N bonds, effectively promoting the separation of photogenerated carriers within Bi2MoO6 and enhancing the photocatalytic activity. NCBMO-3 (mass ratio of N-CDs to Bi2MoO6 is 3 %) achieved 99.7 % degradation efficiency for TCH within 120 min, 1.95 times that of Bi2MoO6. After 5 recycling experiments, NCBMO-3 still had 95 % degradation efficiency for TCH. Compared with the Bi2MoO6-based modified photocatalysts designed in existing literature, NCBMO-3 exhibits more prominent core competitiveness both in terms of photocatalytic activity and cycling stability. NCBMO-3 has a wide range of applicability; the degradation efficiency of ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, phenol, and Rhodamine B exceeded 90 %, and it is robustly resistant to interference from coexisting ions. The photocatalyst prepared in this study has a unique structure and reliable mechanism, which offers an innovative strategy to enhance bismuth-based catalysts' photocatalytic performance by using biomass-derived carbon materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing biomass-derived carbon materials to enhance the photocatalytic degradation activity of bismuth-based catalysts for tetracycline hydrochloride is significant for the efficient utilization of renewable energy. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) were introduced into Bi2MoO6 via a solvothermal approach to construct the N-CDs/Bi2MoO6 (NCBMO) nanoflower spherical photocatalyst, which is abundant in active sites, constructed electron transfer channels at the interface between N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 by forming Bi-O-N bonds, effectively promoting the separation of photogenerated carriers within Bi2MoO6 and enhancing the photocatalytic activity. NCBMO-3 (mass ratio of N-CDs to Bi2MoO6 is 3 %) achieved 99.7 % degradation efficiency for TCH within 120 min, 1.95 times that of Bi2MoO6. After 5 recycling experiments, NCBMO-3 still had 95 % degradation efficiency for TCH. Compared with the Bi2MoO6-based modified photocatalysts designed in existing literature, NCBMO-3 exhibits more prominent core competitiveness both in terms of photocatalytic activity and cycling stability. NCBMO-3 has a wide range of applicability; the degradation efficiency of ofloxacin, ciprofloxacin, phenol, and Rhodamine B exceeded 90 %, and it is robustly resistant to interference from coexisting ions. The photocatalyst prepared in this study has a unique structure and reliable mechanism, which offers an innovative strategy to enhance bismuth-based catalysts' photocatalytic performance by using biomass-derived carbon materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The graphical abstract depicts the reaction process of the NCBMO-3 photocatalyst in the degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH). Under visible light irradiation for 120 min, the degradation efficiency of TCH reaches 99.7 %, with a TOC removal efficiency of 76.26 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic contamination has emerged as a pervasive environmental challenge, with its presence in aquatic systems being particularly concerning [1,2]. The persistence and bioaccumulation of these toxic compounds pose significant risks to both the integrity of ecosystems and the well-being of human beings [3]. Tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH), an extensively utilized broad-spectrum antibiotic, has garnered widespread application in both human medicine and animal husbandry, especially in controlling infectious diseases and promoting animal growth [4]. However, the abuse and improper discharge of containing TCH wastewater by human activities have caused serious aquatic ecosystem pollution problems [5,6]. Conventional methods like adsorption [7], biodegradation [8], and membrane separation [9] have many drawbacks in treating TCH contamination, including insufficient treatment efficiency, high cost, and the possibility of secondary contamination [10,11]. Hence, it is particularly urgent to develop environmentally friendly and efficient antibiotic degradation technologies. Semiconductor photocatalysis, recognized as an advanced oxidation process (AOPs), distinguishes itself through its eco-friendly attributes, recyclability, and energy-efficient nature [12,13]. This technology represents a cost-effective environmental remediation strategy with significant environmental benefits [14–16].

In the realm of semiconductor materials, bismuth-based materials stand out for their extensive application potential in photocatalysis, attributed to their sensitivity to visible spectrum irradiation and distinctive electronic configurations [17]. In contrast to certain rare or expensive elements, these materials are typically more economically viable, thus promoting their scalability for industrial applications and large-scale production [18–20]. It is noteworthy that the Aurivillius compound bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6), with its unique perovskite-type [MoO4]2− and fluorite [Bi2O2]2+ layers alternating layered structure [21], as well as its narrow bandgap (2.5–2.8 eV) conducive to capturing visible light [22,23], is widely used for decomposing organic contaminants [24,25], bacterial inactivation and disinfection [26], and as piezoelectric materials [27]. However, Bi2MoO6 faces two issues that limit its photocatalytic efficiency in practical applications: the first is the swift recombination of electron-hole [28], and secondly, its relatively narrow light response range [23]. Of particular interest, research has demonstrated that the incorporation of carbon quantum dots into bismuth molybdate (Bi2MoO6) through composite modification effectively augments Bi2MoO6's photocatalytic capabilities [29].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with nanometer-scale dimensions (commonly less than 10 nm), have garnered considerable attention for their exceptional electron transfer and storage properties, coupled with a stable chemical nature [30]. Their utility in the modification of semiconductor materials to enhance photocatalytic performance is currently a subject of extensive research [31,32]. It merits emphasis that selecting green biomass as the precursor for the synthesis of CDs not only enriches the oxygen-containing functional groups, enhancing their ability to capture and convey carriers, but also promotes the recycling of biomass waste, thus achieving dual optimization in environmental sustainability and performance efficiency [33,34]. However, the limited number of active sites [35] and weak optical properties [36] of CDs lead to the fact that there is a significant gap in achieving the efficient application of semiconductor catalysts modified with CDs. Currently, doping carbon dots (CDs) with non-metal elements like boron (B) and nitrogen (N), with atomic radii similar to that of carbon (C), provides a powerful means of enhancing the optical properties and modulating the electronic structure of CDs [37,38]. It has been shown that nitrogen doping can significantly enhance the photosensitivity of CDs [39], facilitating the capture of a broader spectrum of photons and thereby extending the light response range of semiconductor photocatalysts. To surmount the limitations impeding the photocatalytic activity of Bi2MoO6, we can utilize nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) to modify Bi2MoO6, thereby establishing numerous effective carrier transfer channels at Bi2MoO6-N-CDs interface. This modification achieves the efficient migration of carriers from Bi2MoO6 to N-CDs, quickening carrier transfer kinetics. Moreover, the conjugated structure within N-CDs facilitates pollutant capture via π-π interactions [40,41], contributing to improving Bi2MoO6's photocatalytic efficiency. Meanwhile, nitrogen doping can introduce a variety of functional groups into CDs, which aids in the tight binding of CDs with Bi2MoO6 and facilitates the creation of additional charge transfer interfacial channels within the composite material. However, achieving this goal necessitates precise control over the degree and distribution of carbon quantum dot doping [42], as well as the optimization of the preparation conditions for the composite material, thereby ensuring the composite exhibits optimal photocatalytic performance. Recently, the role of oxygen in photocatalytic reactions has been highlighted. Huang et al. showed that O2 can dynamically renovate the catalyst structure, enhancing photocatalytic performance by preventing Bi3+ reduction to Bi0 [43], which is particularly relevant to our study using bismuth molybdate-based photocatalysts under ambient conditions.

Herein, N-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal process using waste mulberry branch powder and urea as precursors. Subsequently, N-CDs with various doping mass ratios were deposited onto bismuth molybdate microspheres to obtain a series of N-CDs/Bi2MoO6 (NCBMO) composite photocatalysts. Selecting TCH as the target contaminant, we explored the photocatalytic efficiency of NCBMO in degrading TCH and assessed how different variables affect the degradation efficiency. Building upon existing foundation, the findings from free radical trapping and ESR analyses were examined, leading to the formulation of a hypothesis regarding the photocatalytic mechanism. Additionally, UPLC-MS was used to detect intermediate molecules in the breakdown of TCH, and various potential degradation pathways were inferred. Finally, an evaluation of the toxicity for TCH and its decomposed products was assessed and predicted with the aid of T.E.S.T. In summary, this work aims to provide a green, stable, and efficient modification and preparation strategy to enhance the practical application efficiency of bismuth molybdate photocatalysts.
All chemicals details were showed in Text S1.

5 g dried mulberry branch powder and 3 g urea in 30 mL DI water, sealed, magnetic stirred 2 h to ensure that the solution is evenly mixed. Then, poured the mixture into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined reactor, hydrothermal reaction in oven (180 °C, 14 h). Subsequently, the reaction solution was subjected to centrifugation at a speed of 9000 revolutions per minute for 8 min. The supernatant, after centrifugation, was passed through a 0.22-micrometer filter to eliminate impurities, resulting in a brown solution. The filtered solution was dialyzed for 50 h in a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove low molecular weight impurities, resulting in a purified N-CDs solution. Finally, the N-CDs solution was subjected to freeze-drying, ultimately yielding N-CDs powder under low-temperature protection ( Fig. 1a).
The N-CDs/Bi2MoO6 (NCBMO) composite catalyst was successfully synthesized by solvothermal process (Fig. 1b). More details of the NCBMO composite catalyst preparation was showed in Text S2.
For detailed information on characterizations and photoelectrochemical tests, refer to Text S3 and Text S4.

The efficiency of synthesized catalysts in photocatalysis was assessed by degrading TCH under simulated visible light exposure, and the catalysts were analyzed for their mineralization efficiency of TCH, while the active species involved in the degradation were characterized. The experimental methods is fully detailed in Text S5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathway analysis and toxicity assessment
ce:section.ce:para: The analyze of TCH photocatalytic degradation intermediates was performed using ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS), while a Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T) was employed to forecast potential environmental and aquatic toxicity of these intermediates (see Text S6 for details).

The XRD pattern of the hydrothermally synthesized N-CDs ( Fig. 2a), exhibits a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 22°, signifying an amorphous carbon structure with disordered lattice stacking [39]. The XRD patterns of the NCBMO-X (X = 1, 3, 5) composites and pure Bi2MoO6 were depicted in Fig. 2b. For the Bi2MoO6 sample, distinct diffraction peaks at 10.9°, 28.2°, 32.6°, 36.01°, 47°, 55.5°, 58.4°, 76°, and 77.7° correspond to the (020), (131), (200), (151), (212), (331), (262), (193), and (214) planes of orthorhombic Bi2MoO6 (JCPDS No. 84-0787) [44]. These peaks were characteristic of the Koechlinite phase, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of Bi2MoO6 with an orthorhombic crystal structure [45]. Furthermore, the characteristic peak positions of the NCBMO-X samples (X = 1, 3, 5) were essentially consistent with those of the pure Bi2MoO6, indicating no introduction of additional impurity phases and good combination within the composite material. A concomitant decrease in peak intensities of NCBMO was observed in conjunction with increasing N-CDs doping ratios. This may be due to the low crystallinity of the N-CDs [46], which suppressed the crystal growth of Bi2MoO6, resulting in a diminished intensity of the characteristic peaks for Bi2MoO6. Concurrently, the amorphous structure of N-CDs, their low content, and high dispersion preclude the detection of diffraction peaks attributable to these nanoparticles in the NCBMO composites [40,47,48].

The FTIR spectra detail the surface functional group of N-CDs, Bi2MoO6, and NCBMO-3 (Fig. 2c). N-CDs exhibited a broad peak at 3408 cm⁻¹ due to surface amino N-H and O-H bending vibrations [49,50]. The peaks at 2925, 1634 cm⁻¹ ascribed to the stretching vibrations of aromatic C-H, CN/CC bonds [51]. The presence of conjugated aromatic carbonyl groups was signified by the weak peak at 1410 cm⁻¹, while peaks at 1152, 1044 cm⁻¹ attributed to stretching vibrations of C-NH-C, C-N bonds [52]. The FTIR spectrum of N-CDs showed peaks at 3408 cm⁻¹ (N-H) and 1044 cm⁻¹ (C-N), signifying nitrogen successfully doped into the carbon quantum dot. Based on the aforementioned analysis, the N-CDs surface was enriched with nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups, like amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl. As regards Bi2MoO6, peak at 1626 cm⁻¹ and wide band between 3300 and 3500 cm⁻¹ ascribed to the bending vibrations of hydroxyl groups [53]. Furthermore, the vibrational bands within the range of 500–900 cm⁻¹ were predominantly attributed to the stretching vibrations of Bi-O, asymmetric stretching vibrations of Mo-O, and the bending vibrations of the Mo-O-Mo linkages [54]. The FTIR spectrum of NCBMO-3 was fundamentally aligned with Bi2MoO6, reflecting similar surface functional groups and peak positions, with the additional presence of weak characteristic peaks attributed to N-CDs. This fact substantiated the successful combination of N-CDs with Bi2MoO6. Notably, NCBMO-3 has a new Bi-O-N absorption peak at 970 cm⁻¹ compared to Bi2MoO6, the presence of Bi-O-N bonds confirmed the robust bonding of N-CDs with Bi2MoO6.

Employing SEM and EDS, the morphological and elemental distribution of the materials were investigated, with the corresponding images detailed in Fig. 3. As depicted in Fig. 3a-c, pure Bi2MoO6, during the solvothermal reaction process, undergoes disordered stacking and self-assembly into irregular nanoflower spheres structure with a rough surface and abundant porosity. This unique architecture facilitates the exposure of active sites for photocatalytic reactions. Examination of the SEM micrographs revealed that NCBMO-3 (Fig. 3d-f), akin to Bi2MoO6, maintains an irregular nanoflower spheres morphology, suggesting that the N-CDs doping has not disrupted the Bi2MoO6 morphological structure. Due to the diminutive size of N-CDs, their presence was not discernible in the SEM micrographs. In Fig. 3g-l and Fig. S1, the NCBMO-3 composite material's surface was observed to contain elements Bi, Mo, O, C, and N, with homogeneous elemental distribution. The presence of N and C elements was attributed to the N-CDs, thereby further validating the successful fabrication of the NCBMO-3 composite catalyst.

Utilizing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), an in-depth investigation of the nano-morphology and lattice fringes of N-CDs and NCBMO-3 was conducted, as depicted in Fig. 4. Fig. 4a illustrated the synthesized N-CDs, which appear as black, spherical nanoparticles with particle sizes ranging from 3 to 13 nm. These nanoparticles exhibited excellent dispersibility, with no evidence of aggregation. Furthermore, the particle size distribution, detailed in the inset histogram, delineates an average diameter measurement of 7.267 ± 1.224 nm for the N-CDs. In N-CDs' HRTEM image (Fig. 4b), well-defined lattice fringes with spacing of 0.228 nm were identified, matching the (110) plane of graphite [55], indicative of a crystalline graphite-like carbon structure in the synthesized N-CDs. The TEM image of NCBMO-3 (Fig. 4c) revealed that N-CDs were evenly dispersed on NCBMO-3, as indicated by the yellow circles, and exhibited a close interfacial connection with Bi2MoO6. Fig. 4d displayed the HRTEM image of NCBMO-3, where lattice fringes with 0.313 nm spacing, attributed to the (131) crystallographic plane of Bi2MoO6 [56], and 0.228 nm fringes, matching the (110) plane of N-CDs. The collective findings herein provide further validation for the successful synthesis of the nanoflower spheres NCBMO-3 composite material in this investigation.

The elemental composition and chemical valence states of the materials were characterized via XPS, and detailed in Fig. 5. From Fig. 5a, the XPS survey spectrum indicated that the NCBMO-3 material contained elements Bi, Mo, O, C, and N, with the appearance of additional characteristic peaks for N 1 s and C 1 s not found in pure Bi2MoO6 material, which was indicative of the successful incorporation of N-CDs, and corroborated the results from the EDS elemental mapping. In Fig. 5b, the peaks observed at 157.33 eV and 162.65 eV assignable to the Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2, suggesting that Bi predominant existence in Bi3+ oxidation state [57,58]. The presence of Bi3+ was attributed to the [Bi2O2]2+ cluster ion within the NCBMO-3 composite. Furthermore, NCBMO-3 has two new peaks at 155.83 eV and 161.24 eV, due to the formation of Bi-O-N bonds. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the NCBMO-3 material exhibited two intense peaks at 230.60 eV and 233.75 eV, which correspond to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 spin-orbit split photoelectron peaks [59], respectively. These peaks were indicative of the presence of Mo6+ in the [MoO4]2− tetrahedral anion. Upon examination of Fig. 5d, two distinct characteristic peaks were observed in the O 1 s spectrum, corresponding to the metal-oxygen bonds of Bi-O at 529.16 eV and Mo-O at 530.62 eV [60]. Moreover, a new peak was found at 531.89 eV, which proved the formation of Bi-O-N bonds. It is noteworthy that, in comparison to the pristine Bi2MoO6, the characteristic peaks of Bi 4 f, Mo 3d, and O 1 s in the NCBMO-3 composite exhibited a shift towards lower binding energies, indicative of significant electronic interactions between the N-CDs and Bi2MoO6. In Fig. 5e, the C 1 s spectrum of NCBMO-3 revealed three peaks at 284.80, 286.28, and 288.60 eV, corresponding to sp2 C-C, C-N, and CO bonds [61], respectively. Fig. 5f shows the N 1 s spectrum of NCBMO-3 with peaks at 397.35, 398.81, 399.95, and 400.91 eV, assigned to Bi-O-N, N-H, CN, and OC-N bonds [62]. Upon binding with Bi2MoO6, the characteristic peaks associated with C 1 s and N 1 s of N-CDs were observed to shift towards lower binding energies. The observed shift resulted from electrons being transferred to N-CDs from Bi2MoO6, consequently reducing their binding energies. This finding corroborated the Bi-O-N bond formed between N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 effectively promotes electron transfer, while signifying a tight combination of the two materials. In summary, the XPS analysis further confirmed the results of FTIR analysis.

The characteristics of physical adsorption in materials were crucial factors influencing catalytic reactions. In Fig. S2a, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 exhibited similarities, with the appearance of hysteresis effects within the relative pressure between 0.6 and 1.0 P/P0. The occurrence of hysteresis loops was indicative of Type IV isotherms according to the IUPAC classification [63], accompanied by characteristics of H3 type hysteresis loops, signifying the mesoporous structure of both Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 materials. As observed in Fig. S2b further confirmed the presence of mesoporosity in the materials. Table S1 provided detailed information on the specific surface area, pore volume, average pore diameter for both Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3. The NCBMO-3 material showed a specific surface area of 60.01 m²/g, significantly greater than pristine Bi2MoO6 at 47.58 m²/g. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) can be uniformly dispersed on the surface or within bismuth molybdate, forming numerous contact points and interfaces. These contact points and interfaces increase the specific surface area of the composite material NCBMO-3. Additionally, a larger surface area exposes more active sites, enhancing the contact area between the catalyst and pollutants [64], thereby improving the catalyst's adsorption capacity and photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and band structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra analysis was conducted to assess the light absorption property of each photocatalyst. Fig. 6a displayed the UV–vis DRS spectra for Bi2MoO6 and the NCBMO-X (X = 1, 3, 5), it could be observed that across the spectrum of 200–800 nm, all catalysts exhibited steep absorption curves. Pure Bi2MoO6 demonstrated a distinct light absorption effect within the wavelength edge of 475 nm, aligning with previous research findings [65]. The combination of N-CDs with Bi2MoO6 led to a redshift effect in the catalyst light absorption threshold, with the absorption edge of NCBMO-3 extended to 508 nm, demonstrating the most significant enhancement in visible light absorption property. The enhanced light absorption property of NCBMO-3 implies that it can absorb and utilize a greater number of photons under visible light irradiation, thereby facilitating the generation of more photogenerated electrons and holes that participate in the photocatalytic reactions, which is beneficial for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. This suggests that incorporating N-CDs effectively improves the visible light utilization efficiency of Bi2MoO6. The amelioration was associated with the up-conversion luminescence and strong photosensitivity characteristics of N-CDs [66], which capture a broad spectrum of visible light and make it available for Bi2MoO6. The Kubelka-Munk function (Eq. (1)) [67] has been used to calculate the band gap energy (E
g ) of various materials.
(1) (αhν) n = Α (hν − E
g )

Where α, h, ν, Α, E
g indicate absorption coefficient, Planck's constant, optical frequency, proportionality constant, and band gap energy. Bi2MoO6, classified as a direct band gap semiconductor, n was 2 [68]. Fig. 6b-c presents the Tauc plots for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3, and E
g is obtained by extrapolating the linear part of the curves until the value of the longitudinal coordinate is zero [69]. As illustrated, the E
g value of pristine Bi2MoO6 is 2.88 eV, while the E
g value of the N-CDs incorporated composite, NCBMO-3, was reduced to 2.67 eV.

Additionally, the determination of flat band potentials (E FB) for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 was ascertained through the analysis of Mott-Schottky curves (different frequencies). As shown in Fig. 6d-e, the extrapolation of the tangent of the Mott-Schottky curves (800 and 1000 Hz) to the x-axis, the E FB for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 was obtained, which were determined to be −0.43 V and −0.52 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The E FB of Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) can be calculated by conversion formula (Eq. (2)) [70]. The E FB for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 were calculated as −0.233 eV and −0.323 eV (vs. NHE). Concurrently, the Mott-Schottky curves of both Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 exhibited positive inclinations, signifying their classification as n-type semiconductors [71]. Typically, n-type semiconductors' conduction band potential (E CB) is 0.1–0.3 V more negative than E FB [72], we can estimate the E CB for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 as −0.33 V and −0.42 V. From the Tauc plots, it was concluded that Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 possess E
g of 2.88 eV and 2.67 eV, correspondingly. Utilizing Eq. (3) [73], Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 were calculated to have valence band potentials (E VB) of 2.55 V and 2.25 V. This is in perfect agreement with the results obtained from the valence band XPS energy spectrum (VB-XPS) for Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 (Fig. 6f-g), which strongly confirms that the synthesized catalysts possess accurate band structures. Incorporating the aforementioned analysis, the band structure diagrams of Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 were depicted in Fig. 6h.
(2)
E NHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.197
(3)
E VB = E CB + E g

Photon-generated charge carriers recombination produces photoluminescence emission, whose intensity reflects the extent of charge carrier recombination [74]. Therefore, photoluminescence (PL) experiments could provide insight into the efficiency of transferring and separating photogenerated carriers in various catalysts. Pure Bi2MoO6 demonstrated a predominant emission peak at 435 nm when excited at 360 nm wavelength (Fig. 6i). Moreover, the PL intensity is significantly lower in NCBMO-3 than in Bi2MoO6. This suggests that introducing N-CDs into Bi2MoO6 efficiently suppressed photogenerated carriers' recombination, leading to a substantial enhancement of NCBMO-3's photocatalytic properties. The separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers were further analyzed by time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) spectra (Fig. 6j), and the average lifetime of NCBMO-3 (0.575 ns) was longer than that of pure Bi2MoO6 (0.443 ns), which proved once again that the construction of the composite material effectively improved the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers, allowing more of the photogenerated carriers to participate in the photocatalytic reaction.

Photoelectrochemical tests assessed the efficiency of photogenerated carriers transfer and separation within Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3. Fig. 7 showed the transient photocurrent response curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3. It was evident that during the six light illumination-darkening cycles performed under visible light, both Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 materials exhibited a sensitive increase and decrease in photocurrent response with the initiation and termination of light exposure (Fig. 7a). Specifically, NCBMO-3 demonstrated a higher photocurrent density, reaching up to 10 μA·cm-², which was 1.65 times that of Bi2MoO6 at 6.05 μA·cm-². A higher photocurrent density also signifies that the material has excellent charge transfer capabilities [75,76]. Moreover, throughout the six cyclic periods, the photocurrent intensity of NCBMO-3 remained more stable compared to Bi2MoO6, indicating that NCBMO-3 exhibits a more pronounced stability in exciting and transferring photogenerated charges. Fig. 7b illustrates the impedance spectra of Bi2MoO6 and NCBMO-3 as EIS Nyquist plots. The Nyquist impedance curve arc radius is directly proportional to the resistance encountered during charge transfer, which indicates that a smaller radius is associated with lower resistance and, consequently, higher charge transfer efficiency [77,78]. Clearly, the impedance curve radius for NCBMO-3 was smaller than that for Bi2MoO6, which means that the photogenerated charges encounter less resistance during transfer in NCBMO-3, effectively suppressing photogenerated carriers' recombination. This agrees with analysis results from aforementioned transient photocurrent response curve. The photoelectrochemical test results collectively demonstrated that the Bi-O-N bonds formed between Bi2MoO6 and N-CDs had effectively improved the transfer efficiency of charges, further boosted the photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 8a showed TCH degradation by different catalysts. The blank control group showed no significant degradation after 120 min of visible light exposure, suggesting that TCH is stable under visible light and does not degrade easily under natural conditions. Pure Bi2MoO6 exhibited a TCH degradation efficiency of merely 51.2 %. Under identical conditions, TCH achieved a 61.2 % degradation efficiency catalyzed by the CDs/Bi2MoO6 composite material, representing a modest improvement over the pure Bi2MoO6. Encouragingly, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/bismuth molybdate (NCBMO) photocatalysts notably enhanced TCH degradation, with NCBMO-3 photocatalysts showing a high degradation efficiency of 99.7 %, surpassing Bi2MoO6 and CDs/Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts by about 2 and 1.63 times (Fig. 8c). This enhancement was due to the efficient electron transfer to the interface of N-CDs from Bi2MoO6, which accelerates the charge transfer rate. Furthermore, by observing the temporal absorption spectrum during the TCH photodegradation (Fig. S3), the characteristic peaks of TCH sharply decrease as the reaction time progresses. Additionally, the π-conjugated framework of N-CDs promotes the adherence of TCH molecules via π-π stacking interactions, augmenting the adsorptive and photocatalytic performance of the NCBMO-3 semiconductor. However, it was observed that the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the composite initially rose and then fell as the doping ratio of N-CD increased (Fig. 8c). This is because an appropriate increase in N-CD content promotes the formation of oxygen vacancies, facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers. In contrast, an excess of carbon quantum dots would impede Bi2MoO6's visible light absorption, diminishing the composite's photocatalytic efficiency [47].

Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation process of TCH conformed first-order kinetics, and the rate constant k for each catalytic degradation system was obtained by applying the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 8b). NCBMO-3 (k = 0.03417 min–1) had the largest degradation rate constant, exceeding the control sample CDs/Bi2MoO6 (k = 0.00952 min−1) by 3.6 times, suggesting that adding N-CDs effectively enhances catalyst's photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The mineralization efficiency of TCH by different materials was evaluated through the determination of TOC (Fig. 8d). TOC removal efficiencies of TCH in CDs, N-CDs, Bi2MoO6, CDs/Bi2MoO6, NCBMO-1, NCBMO-3, and NCBMO-5 photocatalytic degradation systems were 0.38 %, 0.52 %, 41.12 %, 56.43 %, 67.29 %, 76.26 %, and 64.21 %, respectively. Notably, the photocatalytic system containing NCBMO-3 exhibited the highest TOC removal efficiency, the result is similar to the degradation reaction outcomes. Compared to the individual components of CDs, N-CDs and Bi2MoO6, the NCBMO composite materials showed enhanced mineralization rates for TCH to varying degrees and NCBMO-3 is the best among them, further confirming the synergy of N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 in improving TCH degradation and mineralization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of different variables on the photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: In real-world practice, fluctuating contaminants concentrations may influence the photocatalytic effect of photocatalysts. Impact of different concentrations of TCH, ranging from 10 to 40 mg/L, influence photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts was investigated ( Fig. 9a). TCH degradation efficiency showed a noticeable decrease from 99.3 % to 73.2 % with gradually increasing TCH solution concentration. The primary reason was that the degradation of TCH necessitates a greater amount of active species as the solution's TCH molecule count rose. With a fixed dosage of NCBMO-3 photocatalyst and under constant light intensity, catalyst's active sites are finite, therefore the amount of active species produced theoretically remains unchanged. Thus, the active species were insufficient for higher concentrations of TCH, and TCH concentration increased, in turn, reduced the photocatalyst's light utilization efficiency [79], resulting in reduced degradation efficiency as TCH concentrations increase. In the following degradation experiments, TCH solution concentration was 10 mg/L.

Fig. 9b shows the impact of the NCBMO-3 catalyst dosage on TCH degradation efficiency. It can be observed that TCH removal efficiency goes up from 45.3 % to 99.3 % when catalyst dosage was increased from 0.1 to 0.5 g/L. This was due to the increased photocatalyst dosage with higher free radical generation, and these reactive free radicals accelerated the removal of TCH. However, upon further increasing the photocatalyst dosage to 0.6 g/L, TCH removal efficiency dropped to 83.2 %. At this point, the reaction solution in the photocatalytic tube became turbid, causing the scattering of visible light within the solution, and hindering the absorption of visible light by NCBMO-3 photocatalyst and resulting in decreased active sites on NCBMO-3 surface [80]. Consequently, this obstruction reduced the photocatalyst's efficiency in removing TCH. In the following degradation experiments, the catalyst dosage of NCBMO-3 was 0.5 g/L.

TCH degradation efficiency is strongly correlated with the pH of the solution, as different pH values can significantly influence on contaminant ionization state and photocatalyst surface charge properties [81]. To this end, TCH degradation efficiency has been studied under initial solution pH conditions of 3, 4.67 (in its natural state), 5, 7, 9, and 11. From Fig. 9c, it could be deduced that the NCBMO-3 exhibited a characteristic of initially increasing and then decreasing degradation efficiency of TCH as solution pH increased. Maximum degradation efficiency of 99.56 % was observed at the natural pH of 4.67. When an increase in pH from 3 to 4.67, TCH degradation efficiency rose from 76.1 % to 99.56 %. Subsequently, upon further raising the pH from 4.67 to 11, the TCH degradation efficiency continuously declined to 50.86 %. The mechanism behind this phenomenon was closely related to the influence of environmental pH on both the ionization state of TCH and the surface charge characteristics of the NCBMO-3 catalyst. It was intriguing to note that TCH exhibited three distinct dissociation constants in solution, namely pK a,1 = 3.30, pK a,2 = 7.68, and pK a,3 = 9.68. This implied that the TCH ionic species varied with solution pH: as a cationic state (H4TC+) at pH < 3.30, as a zwitterionic state (H3TC0) when 3.30 < pH < 7.68, and as anionic states (H2TC-, HTC2-) at pH > 7.68 [82]. As depicted in Fig. 9d, the point of zero charge (pHpzc) of NCBMO-3, determined by the charge titration method (the experimental methods are fully detailed in Text S7), was 5.95. This indicated that when the environmental solution pH was less than 5.95, the NCBMO-3 surface bears a positive charge, and when the environmental solution pH exceeds 5.95, the surface takes on a negative charge. Considering the ionization states of TCH across different pH ranges, electrostatic attraction between NCBMO-3 surface-charged groups and TCH molecules facilitates TCH removal when the environmental pH was within the range of 3.3–7.68. Conversely, when the environmental pH is below 3.30 or above 7.68, like-charged repulsion occurs between the NCBMO-3 surface and TCH molecules. Collectively, under weakly acidic to neutral pH conditions, the NCBMO-3 composite catalyst demonstrates effective photocatalytic degradation of TCH.

In practical applications, various inorganic ions coexist with pollutants in wastewater. Therefore, some typical inorganic ions (Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, H2PO4 −) were added to the reaction solution to investigate their impact on TCH photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 9e). The inorganic anions CO3 2− and H2PO4 − exerts a certain inhibitory effect on TCH photocatalytic degradation. This was due to these coexisting anions scavenging the key substance in the photocatalytic reaction, the free radicals, leading to the formation of less reactive inorganic free radical ions [83]. CO3 2− and H2PO4 − capture photogenerated holes h+ to produce CO3•− and H2PO4•, thereby reducing the oxidative power of the free radicals. Moreover, the presence of the cation Ca2+ also produces a certain inhibitory effect compared to the blank control group. This was because Ca2+ forms metal complexes (Ca(TCH)•nH2O) in the TCH solution, which deposit on the surface of NCBMO-3, blocking the active sites' absorption channels for visible light.

Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation performance of NCBMO-3 on TCH under real aquatic environmental conditions was explored to examine the feasibility of the composite photocatalyst NCBMO-3 in practical applications. For this purpose, we collected lake water (from Cuihu and Biyun Lake within the campus of Guangxi University), pond water (from the Lotus Pond within Guangxi University), the Yongjiang River water (Nanning, Guangxi, China), tap water, and deionized water, respectively, as solvents for preparing TCH solutions. 20 mg NCBMO-3 was dispersed in 40 mL of TCH solution at 10 mg/L mass concentration, and after a dark reaction adsorption for 30 min, the reaction was carried out under visible light for 120 min (Fig. 9f). It could be observed that in the aquatic environments of lake water, pond water, Yongjiang River water, and tap water, the degradation efficiency of TCH was somewhat lower compared to deionized water conditions, because they contain inorganic and organic ions that can capture the active species responsible for TCH degradation [84]. Overall, TCH photocatalytic degradation efficiency by NCBMO-3 under different aquatic environments can all reach more than 90 %, which further proves that NCBMO-3 has good practical application potential.

The lifespan of photocatalysts is essential for real-world applications. Hence, evaluated the cycling stability of the optimal composite catalyst, NCBMO-3. As shown in Fig. 10a, following five consecutive cycles, TCH degradation efficiencies by NCBMO-3 were 99.7 %, 98.9 %, 97.6 %, 96.3 %, and 95 %, respectively, highlighting its excellent cyclic stability. Fig. 10b-c shows the leaching concentrations of Bi3+ and Mo6+ in the solution after each cyclic degradation experiment. The results indicate that the concentrations of Bi3+ and Mo6+ in all post-degradation solutions were below 0.1 mg/L, and the leaching concentrations of Bi3+ and Mo6+ decreased progressively with increasing cycle numbers. Furthermore, compared to the freshly prepared NCBMO-3 catalyst, the types and positions of the characteristic peaks in each XPS spectrum of NCBMO-3 after the reaction (Fig. 10d-h) remain essentially consistent, although the intensities of these peaks have slightly decreased, which may be associated with the loss of ions during the cyclic process. Moreover, comparison of the SEM and TEM images of NCBMO-3 before and after the reaction (Fig. 10i-j) reveals that there is no significant difference in the surface morphology of the photocatalyst, and the lattice fringes of N-CDs and Bi2MoO6 can still be clearly resolved. Additionally, after five cycles, NCBMO-3's XRD peaks retained all the characteristic peaks of the original, with no significant difference in crystal structure (Fig. 10k). These results further confirmed the superior structural stability of NCBMO-3 during the repeated use in photocatalytic degradation processes.

To evaluate the generality of the NCBMO-3 composite material, degradation experiments under visible light irradiation were conducted on common organic pollutants present in water bodies, including ofloxacin (OFX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), phenol, and rhodamine B (Rh B). The NCBMO-3 achieved degradation efficiencies of 92.3 % for OFX, 90 % for CIP, 95.7 % for Phenol, and 91.9 % for Rh B (Fig. 10l). This result demonstrated that NCBMO-3 possessed good degradation performance towards multiple common organic pollutants, thus exhibiting strong general applicability in practical applications.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity and cyclic stability of NCBMO-3 prepared in this study were also compared with other Bi2MoO6-based modified photocatalysts for TCH degradation that have been reported (Table S3). It is obvious that the NCBMO-3 photocatalyst has a significant competitive advantage in terms of higher degradation efficiency and stability under similar experimental conditions, as well as lower catalyst dosage for cost savings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species detection and photocatalytic mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the radical capture experiments, indicated that addition these three scavengers to the system of NCBMO-3 degrading TCH led to degradation efficiencies of 84.7 %, 26.8 %, and 7.6 %, respectively ( Fig. 11a). Compared to the blank control group, the addition of p-BQ (•O2 − scavenger) and EDTA-2Na (h+ scavenger) notably inhibited TCH photocatalytic degradation, suggests that •O2 − and h+ are the primary active species in TCH degradation process, with a more pronounced role for h+; the addition of IPA (•OH scavenger) slightly inhibited TCH photocatalytic degradation suggests that •OH are rarely involved in TCH degradation process and have small a contribution. The results are consistent with those in Fig. 6h band structures schematic diagram.

Additionally, ESR spectra were used to further investigate •O2 − generated in the NCBMO-3 photocatalytic system. No characteristic DMPO-•O2 − signal was detected in the dark as shown in Fig. 11b. Nevertheless, following 10 min of exposure to visible light, the characteristic signal became distinctly observable in the spectra. The ESR test results indicated that the NCBMO-3 material can generate the •O2 − radical under visible light, aligning with the results of the radical capture experiment. This validated that free radicals h+ and •O2 − are the main active species responsible for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH by NCBMO-3 catalyst. From the EPR spectra of DMPO-•OH (Fig. 11c), the DMPO-•OH signal was not detected under both dark and visible light conditions, further indicating that •OH is not the predominant active radical.

Drawing from the above conducted experiments and analytical test results, a mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of TCH using the NCBMO-3 catalyst was inferred. The degradation mechanism was illustrated in Fig. 12. First, the conjugated structure within the N-CDs adsorbed TCH molecules onto the surface of the composite photocatalyst NCBMO-3 through π-π interactions. Subsequently, under visible light irradiation, Bi2MoO6 within the composite material absorbed photon energy, exciting the photogenerated electrons (e-) to transition to the CB of Bi2MoO6, while left an equivalent amount of holes (h+) in the VB of Bi2MoO6 (Eq. (4)) [65]. Concurrently, the N-CDs, endowed with superior electron storage and transfer capabilities, formed the charge transfer interface channel by tightly binding with Bi2MoO6 through Bi-O-N bonds, thereby expedited the rapid transfer of e− from the CB of Bi2MoO6 to the N-CDs (Eq. (5)). This process accelerated the separation of photogenerated carriers on Bi2MoO6, this helped to boost the photocatalyst's activity. Additionally, NCBMO-3's CB potential was −0.42 eV vs. NHE, which was more negative than the redox potential of O2/•O2 − (-0.33 eV vs. NHE). Therefore, the e- accumulated in NCBMO-3's CB and on the surface of N-CDs reacted with dissolved oxygen in water to form superoxide radical anions (•O2 −) (Eq. (6)) [85], then directly attacked TCH molecules, facilitating their mineralization and decomposition into small molecule products (Eq. (7)). At the same time, as e- continue to transfer, a substantial amount of h+ with strong oxidizing properties were retained in the VB of NCBMO-3, which could attack the pollutant TCH molecules, accelerating their catalytic degradation (Eq. (8)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The potential degradation pathways of TCH
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results of the UPLC-MS detected intermediates, three potential degradation pathways for TCH have been deduced, as illustrated in Fig. 13. Information on the main intermediates was presented in Fig. S4 and Table S4 in Supplementary material.

In Pathway I, TCH (m/z = 445) underwent dehydroxylation to form P1 (m/z = 413), which was then attacked by radicals, losing dimethylamino and amide groups, followed by oxidative ring-opening to yield P2 (m/z = 273) [84]. In Pathway II, TCH was demethylated to produce P3 (m/z = 417), which subsequently underwent deamination, dehydroxylation, amide removal, and ring-opening to form P4 (m/z = 277) [86]. Regarding Pathway III, the amide groups of TCH were oxidized by •OH attacked to form P5 (m/z = 418), then P5 further oxidized and underwent ring-opening to give P6 (m/z = 366). Subsequently, the C-C bond in P6 was broken to generate P7 (m/z = 259). The intermediates P2, P4, and P7 from the above pathways were further oxidatively degraded to P8 (m/z = 193), P9 (m/z = 143), P10 (m/z = 145), P11 (m/z = 113), P12 (m/z = 115), and P13 (m/z = 85), which were organic compounds with lower mass-to-charge ratios, and finally mineralized by radical attack to H2O, CO2, and some smaller molecules with lower toxicity.

Based on the UPLC-MS results, it was evident that numerous intermediates were formed during the degradation of TCH, some of which may have had potential ecological toxicity. Therefore, the ecological toxicity of TCH degradation was evaluated using T.E.S.T software based on the principles of Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR). It should be noted that a higher LC50 value indicates lower toxicity, while higher values for developmental toxicity and mutagenicity indicate greater toxicity. Illustrated in Fig. 14a, the LC50 of TCH for fathead minnow was 0.9 mg/L, considered "Toxic". Furthermore, all intermediates LC50 except P1, P2, and P7 were higher than TCH, especially P9, P10, and P12, whose toxicity can be classified as "No harmful". The LC50 of TCH for Daphnia magna was 5.44 mg/L, and it was also deemed "Toxic", similar to the LC50 for fathead minnow (Fig. 14b). The majority of the degradation intermediates exhibit LC50 exceeding that of TCH, with P10 and P12 can be classified as "No harmful", showing that most intermediates have greatly lowered acute toxicity. As depicted in Fig. 14c, the developmental toxicity results indicate that TCH, along with the degradation intermediates, were classified as "Developmental toxicant". However, with the exception of P2, P3, and P7, the developmental toxicity of the intermediates decreased significantly. Moreover, half of the intermediates exhibited mutagenicity negative (Fig. 14d). The foregoing findings robustly demonstrate that the degradation of TCH using the NCBMO-3 photocatalyst was an effective process for reducing ecological toxicity, aligning with our concept of designing an environmentally friendly degradation process.

In summary, a solvothermal method was utilized to deposit N-CDs on the surface of Bi2MoO6, forming numerous robust Bi-O-N bonds that serve as charge transfer channels, effectively facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers; and a novel photocatalyst, N-CDs/Bi2MoO6, which exhibits a stable chemical structure and superior photocatalytic activity, was synthesized. From multiple characterization analysis results, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composite material is attributed to the enhanced light absorption properties, improved separation efficiency of photogenerated charges, enlarged specific surface area, and the exposure of more active sites following the introduction of N-CDs. Photocatalytic degradation experiments demonstrated that the NCBMO-3 material exhibited the most outstanding degradation performance, achieving a degradation efficiency of 99.7 % for TCH in 120 min. The NCBMO-3 composite catalyst showed not only excellent resistance to various environmental factors but also remarkable degradation efficacy against common pollutants in water bodies. Radical capture experiments and ESR tests had indicated that h+ and •O2 − were the main active species responsible for TCH's photocatalytic degradation. The potential degradation pathways for TCH were proposed, and toxicity analysis was conducted. The results indicated that the use of the NCBMO-3 photocatalyst degradation TCH constituted an effective method for reduced ecological toxicity. The distinctive structure and reliable mechanism of the NCBMO composite material offer new strategies for the development of environmentally friendly and potent photocatalysts.
